query: What are the requirements and fees required to submit a valid interconnection application for a large generator in ISO New England, based on the latest ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide and ISO-NE Tariff Schedule 22 Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (LGIP)?
final_answer:
Based on my research of the ISO New England Open Access Transmission Tariff (OATT) Schedule 22 - Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (LGIP), effective May 3, 2025 (ER25-2149-000), and related appendices and compliance filings, here are the requirements and fees for submitting a valid interconnection application for a large generator:

## **Requirements for a Valid Interconnection Request (Section 3.4 of LGIP)**

Per the LGIP and Appendix 1 (Interconnection Request form), a valid Interconnection Request must include:

### **1. Completed Interconnection Request Form (Appendix 1)**
- **Project Information**: Proposed project name, type of request (new facility, capacity increase, market participation commencement, or service type change)
- **Type of Interconnection Service**: Must select either:
  - **Network Resource Interconnection Service (NRIS)** - energy capability only
  - **Capacity Network Resource Interconnection Service (CNRIS)** - energy and capacity capability
- **Facility Details**: 
  - Address/location (town/city, county, state)
  - Requested Point of Interconnection (POI)
  - Generating facility type and fuel type
  - Capacity data at various temperatures (maximum gross/net MW output, net MW capability at POI)
  - Requested Commercial Operation Date, Initial Synchronization Date, In-Service Date
  - Equipment configuration description (# of units, GSUs, control technologies)
  - For storage: charging capability, reactive capability, primary frequency response range

### **2. Site Control Evidence (Section 3.4.2/3.4.3)**
**For CNRIS requests**: 100% exclusive Site Control in Interconnection Customer's name must be provided.

**For NRIS requests**: Either:
- 100% exclusive Site Control in Interconnection Customer's name, **OR**
- In lieu of Site Control: 
  - A **$10,000/MW deposit** (minimum $500,000, maximum $2,000,000), refundable within the cure period per Section 3.4.3
  - A signed officer affidavit stating Site Control is unobtainable due to Regulatory Limitations
  - Documentation describing the source and effects of such Regulatory Limitations

**For modifications to existing facilities**: Certification that Site Control exists and no additional real property is needed.

### **3. Customer Information**
- Company name, ISO Customer ID#, address, representative contact details
- Authorized signature, name, title, date

### **4. Cluster Request Window Timing**
Per Order No. 2023 compliance, Interconnection Requests can **only be submitted during a Cluster Request Window**. The next window is expected **October 5, 2026 – November 19, 2026** (per ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide).

---

## **Fees and Deposits Required**

### **1. Study Deposit (Section 3.1.1.1)**
- **Amount**: Defined in Section 3.1.1.1 of the LGIP (specific dollar amount per MW or fixed amount - the current LGIP references this section but the exact amount is in the tariff text)
- **Purpose**: Funds the Cluster Study
- **Payment**: Must be delivered to System Operator's bank account by electronic transfer (cash only for study deposits)

### **2. Commercial Readiness Deposit (Section 3.4.2(vi))**
- **Amount**: **Equal to two times (2×) the study deposit** described in Section 3.1.1.1
- **Form**: 
  - Irrevocable Letter of Credit from a financial institution on the ISO's List of Eligible Letter of Credit Issuers, **OR**
  - Cash (treated per Section 3.7 withdrawal provisions)
- **Requirements**: Must specify the Interconnection Customer name and Interconnection Request
- **Refund**: Refunded to Interconnection Customer according to Section 3.7 of the LGIP

### **3. Interconnection Facilities Study Deposit (Section 8.1 / Appendix 3)**
- **Amount**: Greater of **25% of estimated Interconnection Facilities Study cost** or **$250,000**
- **Plus**: Commercial Readiness Deposit per Section 8.1
- **Payment**: Applied toward actual study costs; difference refunded or billed
- **Monthly invoicing**: Actual costs invoiced monthly, payable within 30 calendar days

### **4. LGIA Deposit (Order No. 2023 requirement)**
- Required upon execution of the Large Generator Interconnection Agreement (post-study phase)

---

## **Key Process Changes Under Order No. 2023 Compliance**

1. **Cluster Study Process**: Replaces serial studies; requests only accepted during Cluster Request Windows
2. **Increased Financial Commitments**: Higher study deposits, Commercial Readiness Deposits (2× study deposit), and LGIA deposits
3. **Site Control Requirements**: Stricter for CNRIS (100% exclusive required); NRIS has deposit alternative
4. **Withdrawal Penalties**: Failure to meet deadlines or cure deficiencies results in withdrawal per Section 3.7
5. **Letter of Credit Options**: Available for Commercial Readiness Deposits (not for study deposits which require cash)

---

## **Sources**

1. **ISO-NE OATT Schedule 22 LGIP** (Effective 5/3/25, ER25-2149-000) - https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/regulatory/tariff/sect_2/sch22/sch_22_lgip.pdf
   - Sections 3.1, 3.2, 3.4 (Valid Interconnection Request), 3.7 (Withdrawal), 8.1 (Facilities Study Agreement)

2. **Appendix 1 - Interconnection Request Form** - https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100030/oatt-schedule-22-appendix-1-large-generator-lgip-interconnection-request-with-attachments.pdf
   - Site control requirements, deposit amounts ($10,000/MW, $500k-$2M range), project data requirements

3. **Appendix 3 - Interconnection Facilities Study Agreement** - https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100030/oatt-schedule-22-appendix-3-large-generator-lgip-interconnection-facilities-study-agreement.pdf
   - Section 5.0: Commercial Readiness Deposit + greater of 25% estimated study cost or $250,000

4. **Order No. 2023 Compliance Presentations** (Dec 2023, Feb 2024) - https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100006/a06_12212023_order_2023_compliance_december_tc_summary_presentation.pdf and https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100008/a03b_2024_02_15_tc_order2023_redlines_update_presentation.pdf
   - Commercial Readiness Deposit = 2× study deposit (Section 3.4.2(vi))
   - Letter of Credit eligibility for Commercial Readiness Deposits
   - Cluster Request Window timing

5. **ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide** - https://www.iso-ne.com/genrtion_resrcs/nwgen_inter/index.html
   - Next Cluster Request Window: October 5 – November 19, 2026

---

**Note**: The exact study deposit amount per MW is specified in Section 3.1.1.1 of the current LGIP tariff text. Developers should consult the most recent posted version on the ISO-NE website and the Interconnection Request Tracking Tool (IRTT) for current values and any updates.

steps.reasoning: Let me search for the specific ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide and the Schedule 22 LGIP. The most relevant filing appears to be ER24-2007 and ER24-2009 which are the Order 2023 compliance filings. Let me also search for the specific "Interconnection Process Guide" document.

steps.content: Calling search_web

tool_input.query: ISO New England Interconnection Process Guide Large Generator Interconnection Procedures LGIP Schedule 22 requirements fees application

steps.content:
{

    {
      "url": "https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100030/oatt-schedule-22-appendix-11-large-generator-lgip-interconnection-agreement.pdf",
      "title": "Article 1 Definitions Article 2 Effective Date, Term and Termination Article 3 Regulatory Filings Article 4 Scope of Service Article 5 Interconnection Facilities Engineering, Procurement, and Construction Article 6 Testing and Inspection Article 7 Metering Article 8 Communications Article 9 Operations Article 10 Maintenance Article 11 Performance Obligation Article 12 Invoice Article 13 Emergencies Article 14 Regulatory Requirements and Governing Law Article 15 Notices Article 16 Force Majeure Article 17 Default Article 18 Indemnity, Consequential Damages and Insurance Article 19 Assignment Article 20 Severability Article 21 Comparability Article 22 Confidentiality Article 23 Environmental Releases Article 24 Information Requirements Article 25 Information Access and Audit Rights Article 26 Subcontractors Article 27 Disputes Article 28 Representations, Warranties and Covenants",
      "author": "McDonald, Patricia",
      "text": "APPENDIX 11 LARGE GENERATOR INTERCONNECTION AGREEMENT\n\nTABLE OF CONTENTS\n\nArticle 1 Definitions \nArticle 2 Effective Date, Term and Termination \nArticle 3 Regulatory Filings \nArticle 4 Scope of Service \nArticle 5 Interconnection Facilities Engineering, Procurement, and Construction \nArticle 6 Testing and Inspection \nArticle 7 Metering \nArticle 8 Communications \nArticle 9 Operations \nArticle 10 Maintenance \nArticle 11 Performance Obligation \nArticle 12 Invoice \nArticle 13 Emergencies \nArticle 14 Regulatory Requirements and Governing Law \nArticle 15 Notices \nArticle 16 Force Majeure \nArticle 17 Default \nArticle 18 Indemnity, Consequential Damages and Insurance \nArticle 19 Assignment \nArticle 20 Severability \nArticle 21 Comparability \nArticle 22 Confidentiality \nArticle 23 Environmental Releases \nArticle 24 Information Requirements \nArticle 25 Information Access and Audit Rights \nArticle 26 Subcontractors \nArticle 27 Disputes \nArticle 28 Representations, Warranties and Covenants \n\nArticle 29 Omitted Article 30 Miscellaneous\n\nTHIS STANDARD LARGE GENERATOR INTERCONNECTION AGREEMENT (“Agreement”) is made and entered into this ____ day of ________ 20__, by and between ________________, a ________________ organized and existing under the laws of the State/Commonwealth of ________________ (“Interconnection Customer” with a Large Generating Facility), ISO New England Inc., a non-stock corporation organized and existing under the laws of the State of Delaware (“System Operator”), and ________________, a ________________ organized and existing under the laws of the State/Commonwealth of ________________ (“Interconnecting Transmission Owner”). Under this Agreement, the Interconnection Customer, System Operator, and Interconnecting Transmission Owner each may be referred to as a “Party” or collectively as the “Parties.”\n\nRECITALS\n\nWHEREAS, System Operator is the central dispatching agency provided for under the Transmission Operating Agreement (“TOA”) which has responsibility for the operation of the New England Control Area from the System Operator control center and the administration of the Tariff; and\n\nWHEREAS, Interconnecting Transmission Owner is the owner or possessor of an interest in the Administered Transmission System; and\n\nWHEREAS, Interconnection Customer intends to own, lease and/or control and operate the Generating Facility identified as a Large Generating Facility in Appendix C to this Agreement; and\n\nWHEREAS, System Operator, Interconnection Customer and Interconnecting Transmission Owner have agreed to enter into this Agreement for the purpose of interconnecting the Large Generating Facility to the Administered Transmission System.\n\nNOW, THEREFORE, in consideration of and subject to the mutual covenants contained herein, it is agreed:\n\nWhen used in this Standard Large Generator Interconnection Agreement, terms with initial capitalization that are not defined in Article 1 shall have the meanings specified in the Article in which they are used.\n\nARTICLE 1. DEFINITIONS The definitions contained in this Article 1 and those definitions embedded in an Article of this Agreement are intended to apply in the context of the generator interconnection process provided for in Schedule 22 (and its appendices). To the extent that the definitions herein are different than those contained in Section I.2.2 of the Tariff, the definitions provided below shall control only for purposes of generator interconnections under Schedule 22. Capitalized terms in Schedule 22 that are not defined in this Article 1 shall have the meanings specified in Section I.2.2 of the Tariff.\n\nAdministered Transmission System shall mean the PTF and the Non-PTF.\n\nAdverse System Impact shall mean any significant negative effects on the stability, reliability or operating characteristics of the electric system.\n\nAffected Party shall mean the entity that owns, operates or controls an Affected System, or any other entity operating outside of the New England Control Area that otherwise may be a necessary party to the interconnection process.\n\nAffected System shall mean any electric system that is outside the New England Control Area that may be affected by the proposed interconnection.\n\nAffected System Facilities Construction Agreement shall mean the agreement contained in Appendix 9 to this LGIP that is made between Interconnecting Transmission Owner and Affected System Interconnection Customer to facilitate the construction of and to set forth cost responsibility for necessary Affected System Network Upgrades on the New England Transmission System.\n\nAffected System Interconnection Customer shall mean any entity that submits an interconnection request for a generating facility to a transmission system outside of the New England Control Area that may cause the need for Affected System Network Upgrades on the New England Transmission System.\n\nAffected System Network Upgrades shall mean the additions, modifications, and upgrades to New England Transmission System required to accommodate Affected System Interconnection Customer’s proposed interconnection to a transmission system other than New England Transmission System.\n\nAffected System Operator shall mean the entity that operates an Affected System.\n\nAffected System Queue Position shall mean the queue position of an Affected System Interconnection Customer in System Operator’s interconnection queue relative to System Operator’s Interconnection Customers’ Queue Positions.\n\nAffected System Study shall mean the evaluation of Affected System Interconnection Customers’ proposed interconnection(s) to a transmission system outside the New England Control Area that have an impact on the New England’s Transmission System, as described in Section 9 of this LGIP.\n\nAffected System Study Agreement shall mean the agreement contained in Appendix 7 to this LGIP that is made between System Operator and Affected System Interconnection Customer to conduct an External Affected System Study pursuant to Section 9 of this LGIP.\n\nAffected System Study Report shall mean the report issued following completion of an Affected System Study pursuant to Section 9.6 of this LGIP.\n\nAffiliate shall mean, with respect to a corporation, partnership or other entity, each such other corporation, partnership or other entity that directly or indirectly, through one or more intermediaries, controls, is controlled by, or is under common control with, such corporation, partnership or other entity.\n\nApplicable Laws and Regulations shall mean all duly promulgated applicable federal, state and local laws, regulations, rules, ordinances, codes, decrees, judgments, directives, or judicial or administrative orders, permits and other duly authorized actions of any Governmental Authority.\n\nApplicable Reliability Standards shall mean the requirements and guidelines of NERC, the NPCC and the New England Control Area, including publicly available local reliability requirements of Interconnecting Transmission Owners or other Affected Parties or Internal Affected Party.\n\nAt-Risk Expenditure shall mean money expended for the development of the Generating Facility that cannot be recouped if the Interconnection Customer were to withdraw the Interconnection Request for the Generating Facility. At-Risk Expenditure may include, but is not limited to, money expended on: (i) costs of federal, state, local, regional and town permits, (ii) Site Control, (iii) site-\n\nspecific design and surveys, (iv) construction activities, and (v) non-refundable deposits for major equipment components. For purposes of this definition, At-Risk Expenditure shall not include costs associated with the Interconnection Studies.\n\nBase Case shall have the meaning specified in Section 2.3 of the Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (“LGIP”).\n\nBase Case Data shall mean the Base Case power flow, short circuit, and stability data bases used for the Interconnection Studies by the System Operator, Interconnection Customer, Intercon",

        "22 Confidential\n[...]\nArticle 2\n[...]\nArticle\n[...]\nTHIS STANDARD LARGE GENERATOR INTERCONNECTION AGRE\n[...]\n(“Agreement”)\n[...]\nmade and entered into this ____\n[...]\nof ________\n[...]\nthe laws of the State/Commonwealth of ________________ (“\n[...]\nconnection Customer” with a Large Generating Facility), ISO New\n[...]\nnon-stock corporation organized and\n[...]\nof the State of Delaware (“System Operator”),\n[...]\nARTICLE 1. DEFINITIONS The definitions contained in this Article 1 and those definitions embedded in an Article of this Agreement are intended to apply in the context of the generator interconnection process provided for in Schedule 22 (and its appendices). To the extent that the definitions herein are different than those contained in Section I.2.2 of the Tariff, the definitions provided below shall control only for purposes of generator interconnections under Schedule 22. Capitalized terms in Schedule 22 that are not defined in this Article 1 shall have the meanings specified in Section I.2.2 of the Tariff.\n[...]\nAt-Risk Expenditure shall mean money expended for the development of the Generating Facility that cannot be recouped if the Interconnection Customer were to withdraw the Interconnection Request for the Generating Facility. At-Risk Expenditure may include, but is not limited to, money expended on: (i) costs of federal, state, local, regional and town permits, (ii) Site Control, (iii) site-\n[...]\nspecific design and surveys, (iv) construction activities, and (v) non-refundable deposits for major equipment components. For purposes of this definition, At-Risk Expenditure shall not include costs associated with the Interconnection Studies.\n[...]\nBase Case shall have the meaning specified in Section 2.3 of the Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (“LGIP”)."

    {
      "url": "https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100008/a03b_2024_02_15_tc_order2023_redlines_update_presentation.pdf",
      "title": "Order No. 2023 – Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements",
      "text": "FEBRUARY 15, 2024 | NEPOOL TRANSMISSION COMMITTEE | DOUBLETREE HOTEL, MILFORD, M A\n\nAl McBride D I R E C T O R | T R A N S M I S S I O N S E R V I C E S & R E S O U R C E Q U A L I F I C A T I O N\n\n#### Summary of Tariff Redline Updates since the January TC Meeting\n\n## Order No. 2023 – Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements\n\nISO-NE PUBLIC 2\n\n### Presentation Overview\n\n• ISO has posted an updated draft of all Tariff redlines associated with Order No. 2023 compliance \n• This presentation summarizes the major updates to the Tariff redlines since the January TC meeting \n• Focus on Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (LGIP) in Schedule 22 of the Open Access Transmission Tariff (OATT) – The Small Generator Interconnection Procedures (SGIP) in Schedule 23 of the OATT have been overhauled to mirror the LGIP – The Elective Transmission Upgrade Interconnection Procedures (ETUIP) in Schedule 25 of the OATT also closely match the LGIP \n\nISO-NE PUBLIC ISO-NE INTERNAL USE\n\n### UPDATES TO THE TARIFF REDLINES SINCE THE\n\n### JANUARY TC MEETING\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 3.1\n\nCommercial Readiness Deposits that must be submitted to the System Operator under this LGIP must be (a) delivered to the System Operator’s bank account by electronic transfer, or (b) through the provision and maintenance of an irrevocable lLetter of cCredit in a form and from a financial institution acceptable to System Operator and included on the List of Eligible Letter of Credit Issuers, as described on the System Operator’s public website, or (c) a combination thereof. Each letter of credit must specify the Interconnection Request to which it corresponds. All costs associated with obtaining a letter of credit shall be borne by the Interconnection Customer. In the event that System Operator identifies an administrative deficiency with a submitted letter of credit, Interconnection Customer shall have five (5) Business Days to cure the deficiency..\n\nIn response to stakeholder suggestion, a cure period has been included for certain Letter of Credit (LOC) issues. Note that an LOC, in the correct amount, must have been submitted by the relevant deadline\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 3.1\n\nIf the System Operator removes the financial institution from the list, Interconnection Customer shall have five (5) Business Days from the date on which System Operator provides notice of such removal to replace the letter of credit with a letter of credit from a financial institution on the list. The System Operator may extend this cure period in its sole discretion. Failure to cure a deficiency within the periods prescribed in this Section 3.1 shall result in the withdrawal of the Interconnection Request pursuant to Section 3.7 of the LGIP without further opportunity to cure.\n\nAdded cure period for certain LOC issues. Note that an LOC, in the correct amount, must have been submitted by the relevant deadline\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Definitions\n\nTransitional Capacity Network Resource Group Study (“Transitional CNR Group Study”) shall mean the study performed by the System Operator under Section III.13.1.1.2.3A of the Tariff and Section 5.1.1.3 of this LGIP.\n\nDefinition for the Transitional Capacity Network Resource Group Study\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 3.2.1.3\n\nAny Interconnection Customer with a valid Interconnection Request for CNR Interconnection Service that has a completed Interconnection System Impact Study as of May 1, 2024, but that has not received a Capacity Supply Obligation through eighteenth Forward Capacity Auction or an earlier auction may: 1) seek to complete the process for obtaining CNR Interconnection Service through the process described in Section III.13.1.1.2A of the Tariff or 2) seek to complete the process for obtaining CNR Interconnection Service through the Transitional Cluster Study. Notwithstanding any other provision of the Tariff, an Interconnection Customer may seek to participate in both the process described in Section III.13.1.1.2A of the Tariff and the Transitional Cluster Study simultaneously. If the Interconnection Customer achieves CNR Interconnection Service through Section III.13.1.1.2A, it may withdraw from the Transitional Cluster Study without penalty and be refunded any remaining study deposits associated with the Transitional Cluster Study. If the Interconnection Customer does not enter, or complete, the process described in either Section III.13.1.1.2A or the Transitional Cluster Study, the System Operator shall reduce the Interconnection Customer’s Interconnection Request to NR Interconnection Service.\n\nIn response to Stakeholder suggestion, captures opportunities to establish CNRIS through the Transitional CNR Group Study or Transitional Cluster\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 5.1.1.3\n\nIn accordance with Section III.13.1.1.2.3A, System Operator shall conduct a Transitional CNR Group Study following the effective date of this LGIP. An Interconnection Customer with an assigned Queue Position as of thirty (30) Calendar Days after April 1, 2024 may participate in the Transitional CNR Group Study, and consistent with Section II.48 of the Tariff, achieve CNR Interconnection Service. Any Interconnection Customer seeking to establish CNR Interconnection Service through this study must (1) have a valid Interconnection Request seeking CNR Interconnection Service, (2) submit a New Capacity Show of Interest Form to participate in the interim reconfiguration auction qualification process, (3) have not secured a Capacity Supply Obligation in the eighteenth Forward Capacity Auction or earlier, (4) have a completed System Impact Study or Interconnection Agreement establishing NR Interconnection Service, and 5) have a Commercial Operation Date prior to June 1, 2028.\n\nEligibility for Transitional CNR Group Study\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 5.1.1.3\n\nSystem Operator shall conduct the study by performing an overlapping impacts analysis in the manner used for CNR Group Studies conducted prior to the effective date of this LGIP and as described in ISO Section III.13.1.1.2.3A and the ISO New England Planning Procedures. The Transitional CNR Group Study shall assure that the Interconnection Customer’s Large Generating Facility can be interconnected in a manner that ensures intra-zonal deliverability by avoidance of the redispatch of other Capacity Network Resources and Elective Transmission Upgrades with CNI Interconnection Service, in accordance with the CC Interconnection Standard and as detailed in the ISO New England Planning Procedures.\n\nScope of Transitional CNR Group Study\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 5.1.1.3\n\nInterconnection Requests for CNR Interconnection Service and CNI Interconnection Service submitted to the System Operator pursuant to Schedules 22, 23, or 25 of Section II of the Tariff and interconnection requests submitted to Interconnecting Transmission Owners or their distribution company affiliates pursuant to the applicable state tariff, rules or procedures that have obtained Proposed Plan Application approval from the System Operator pursuant to Section I.3.9 of the Tariff shall be included in the Transitional CNR Group Study in order of submission/approval (the dates of submission shall be used for Interconnection Requests submitted to the System Operator and the dates of Proposed Plan Application approval shall be used for interconnection requests submitted to Interconnecting Transmission Owners or their distribution company affiliates).\n\nScope of Transitional CNR Group Study, continued\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 5.1.1.3\n\nInterconnection requests submitted to Interconnecting Transmission Owners or their distribution company a",

      "text": "UNITED STATES OF AMERICA BEFORE THE FEDERAL ENERGY REGULATORY COMMISSION\n\nImprovements to Generator ) \nInterconnection Procedures and ) Docket No. RM22-14-000 \nAgreements ) \n\nINITIAL COMMENTS OF ISO NEW ENGLAND INC.\n\nISO New England Inc. (“ISO-NE” or “ISO”) respectfully submits these comments in response to the Notice of Proposed Rulemaking on Improvements to Generator Interconnection\n\nProcedures and Agreements that the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission’s (“Commission”)\n\nissued on June 16, 2022, in the above-captioned proceeding. 1\n\nI. EXECUTIVE SUMMARY\n\nThe Commission’s proposed rule is the second effort stemming from its July 15, 2021\n\nAdvanced Notice of Proposed Rulemaking, 2 which sought to examine the need for reforms in regional transmission planning and generator interconnection processes established in Order Nos.\n\n1\n\nSee Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements, 179 FERC ¶ 61,194 (2022) (“NOPR”). New England’s Interconnection Procedures are contained in Schedules 22, 23 and 25 of Section II of the ISO New England Transmission, Markets and Services Tariff (“Tariff”), which contains the Open Access Transmission Tariff (“OATT”). Schedule 22 contains the Large Generator Interconnection Procedures and Interconnection Agreement (“Schedule 22” or “LGIP”); Schedule 23 contains the Small Generator Interconnection Procedures and Interconnection Agreement (“Schedule 23” or “SGIP”), and Schedule 25 contains the Elective Transmission Upgrade Interconnection Procedures and Interconnection Agreement (“Schedule 25” or “ETU IP”).\n\n2\n\nBuilding for the Future Through Electric Regional Transmission Planning and Cost Allocation and Generator Interconnection, Advance Notice of Proposed Rulemaking, 176 FERC ¶ 61,024 (July 15, 2021) (“ANOPR”); Initial Comments of ISO New Inc., Docket No. RM21-17-000 (Oct. 12, 2021) (“ANOPR Comments”).\n\n2003, 890, and 1000. 3 In the NOPR, the Commission proposes to: “(1) implement a first-ready, first-served cluster study process, (2) increase the speed of interconnection queue processing, and (3) incorporate technological advancements into the interconnection process,” for the Large\n\nGenerating Facilities. 4 The Commission further proposes to “incorporate alternative transmission technologies into the interconnection process and to provide modeling and performance requirements for non-synchronous generators” for Small Generating Facilities. 5\n\nIn New England, the states have set aggressive decarbonization goals, which are expected to drive the dramatic transformation of the resource mix in the region from conventional generators to renewable resources, and increase demand through the electrification of the economy. 6\n\nAchieving this clean energy transition safely and reliably depends, in part, on an efficient interconnection process. Based on the ISO’s interconnection queue as of March 2022, the future resource mix proposed to meet the increasing demand comprises 96% renewable resources, totaling approximately 30,000 MW, including approximately 20,000 MW of wind facilities. 7\n\nThanks to significant Tariff improvements proposed jointly with the New England Participant\n\nTransmission Owners (“PTOs”) and the New England Power Pool (“NEPOOL”), and supported by the New England states, the region’s existing Interconnection Procedures are facilitating\n\n3\n\nStandardization of Generator Interconnection Agreements and Procedures, 104 FERC ¶ 61,103 (2003) (“Order No. 2003”); Preventing Undue Discrimination and Preference in Transmission Service, 118 FERC ¶ 61,119 (2007) (“Order No. 890”); Transmission Planning and Cost Allocation by Transmission Owning and Operating Public Utilities, 136 FERC ¶ 61,051 (2011) (“Order No. 1000”).\n\n4\n\nNOPR at P 4.\n\n5\n\nId. at P 5.\n\n6\n\nFor additional details, see ISO New England Inc.’s 2022 Regional Electric Outlook, available on the ISO NE website at https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/2022/06/2022_reo.pdf (“2022 REO”).\n\n7\n\nSee 2022 REO at 11, 15.\n\ndevelopment of significant amounts of renewable generation. The ISO, however, agrees with the\n\nCommission’s statements in the NOPR that the process can be more efficient. 8\n\nAs discussed in Sections III and IV below, many of the interconnection reforms that ISO\n\nNE has implemented since Order Nos. 2003 and 2006 are consistent with the spirit of the\n\nCommission’s proposed interconnection reforms in the NOPR. These include improvements to integrate the interconnection queue with the Forward Capacity Market (“FCM”), incorporate\n\nElective Transmission Upgrades (“ETU”) into the interconnection process, and establish a limited\n\ncluster study process.\n\nAccordingly, ISO-NE respectfully requests that the Commission not adopt uniform or prescriptive compliance requirements that fail to account for these regional variations in any final rule issued in this proceeding. Instead, the ISO requests that the Commission recognize existing regional differences and allow the necessary flexibility for the ISO to develop a compliance approach that builds on the region’s accomplishments, consistent with the Commission’s long standing principles. This will allow for incorporation of the elements proposed by the Commission into existing processes and procedures in a way that best meets the needs of the region.\n\nWith respect to the specific reforms proposed in the NOPR, ISO-NE offers the following\n\ncomments, discussed in further detail below:\n\n The NOPR appropriately recognizes that significant trade-offs are involved when \npursuing a change as expansive as the first-ready, first-served construct proposed. \nUnder the proposal, ISO-NE’s study efforts would be focused on fewer projects \nthat are more likely to be constructed. However, this trade-off must be realistic. \n Higher study completion expectations and the elimination of the reasonable efforts \nstandard would require that study effort be focused on projects that are sufficiently \nviable, necessitate less flexibility for midstream project changes/ability to consider \nalternatives, and require stricter expectations for the completion of all aspects of \nthe process, such as the preparation of transmission upgrade cost-estimates. \n\nSee e.g. NOPR at PP 3, 22.\n\n New England has made several enhancements to the Interconnection Procedures in \nrecent years including the integration of the interconnection process with the FCM, \nthe inclusion of a process for ETUs, and targeted clustering. The Commission \nshould respect these enhancements in any final rule and allow the ISO to build upon \nthem for compliance purposes. \n The first-ready, first-served construct should apply to all interconnection \nprocedures, not just to large generators, to avoid the operation of multiple, separate \nqueues with fundamentally different expectations in terms of studies and \ntimeframes. \n\nII. COMMUNICATIONS\n\nISO-NE is the independent, private, nonprofit entity that serves as the Regional\n\nTransmission Organization (“RTO”) for New England. The ISO operates the New England bulk power system and administers New England’s organized wholesale electricity market pursuant to the Tariff and the Transmission Operating Agreement (“TOA”) with the New England\n\nParticipating Transmission Owners (“PTO”). As the RTO, the ISO has the responsibility to protect the short-term reliability of the New England Control Area and to plan and operate the system according to reliability standards established by the ISO, the Northeast Power Coordinating\n\nCouncil, Inc. (“NPCC”) and the North American Electric Reliability Corporation (“NERC”).\n\nCorrespondence and communications in this proceeding should be addressed to:\n\nGraham Jesmer \nISO New England Inc. \nOne Sullivan Road \nHolyoke, MA 01040-2841 \nTel: (413) 540-4557 \nFax: (413) 535-4379 \nE-mail: gjesmer@iso-ne.com \n\nIII. BACKGROUND\n\nA. Basis for the NOPR\n\nThe NOPR seeks to advance the efforts the Commission initiated through the ANOPR to update its existing interconnection policies to bring resources on",

      "text": "April 15, 2026\n\nVIA ELECTRONIC FILING\n\nDebbie-Anne A. Transmission, Markets and Services Tariff (“Tariff”). Section II of the Tariff contains the ISO-NE Open Access Transmission Tariff (“OATT”). Schedule 22 of the OATT contains the pro forma Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (“LGIP”) and Large Generator Interconnection Agreement (“LGIA”).\n\n(1) a Standard Large Generator Interconnection Agreement by and among the ISO, as the System Operator, Versant, as the Interconnecting Transmission Owner, Evergreen Wind Power III, LLC (“Evergreen III”), as the Interconnection Customer, and Evergreen Gen Lead, LCC (“Gen Lead Owner”), as the entity that will own certain Interconnection Facilities through which Evergreen III’s Large Generating Facility connects to the Administered Transmission System (“Evergreen III LGIA”); 6\n\n(2) a Standard Large Generator Interconnection Agreement by and among the ISO, as the System Operator, Versant, as the Interconnecting Transmission Owner, Stetson Holdings, LLC (“Stetson Holdings”), as the Interconnection Customer, and Evergreen Gen Lead, LCC (“Gen Lead Owner”), as the entity that will own certain Interconnection Facilities through which Stetson Holdings’ Large Generating Facility connects to the Administered Transmission System (“Stetson Holdings LGIA”); 7 and\n\n(3) a Standard Large Generator Interconnection Agreement by and among the ISO, as the System Operator, Versant, as the Interconnecting Transmission Owner, Stetson Wind II, LLC (“Stetson II”), as the Interconnection Customer, and Evergreen Gen Lead, LCC (“Gen Lead Owner”), as the entity that will own certain Interconnection Facilities through which Stetson II’s Large Generating Facility connects to the Administered Transmission System (“Stetson II LGIA”). 8\n\nAs further discussed in Section III below, the previously-effective LGIAs for the three Large Generating Facilities are being replaced as a result of 1) each Large Generating Facility reestablishing Capacity Resource Network Interconnection Service (“CNRIS”) and 2) a change in ownership for the units. The agreements, which are replacing: First Revised Service Agreement No. LGIA-ISONE/BHE-07-01; Original Service Agreement No. LGIA-ISONE/BHE-09-02, and Original Service Agreement No. LGIA-ISONE/BHE-09-03 respectively, 9 are fully executed and\n\n5\n\nSection 11.3 of the LGIP provides that “the System Operator and Interconnecting Transmission Owner shall jointly file the executed LGIA, or amendments thereto, with the Commission under Section 205 of the Federal Power Act.” Consistent with Section 11.3 of the LGIP, the Filing Parties jointly file herein the LGIAs pursuant to Section 205 of the FPA\n\n6\n\nThe Evergreen III LGIA has been designated as Original Revised Service Agreement No. LGIA ISONE/VERSANT-26-01 under the ISO Tariff.\n\n7\n\nThe Stetson Holdings LGIA has been designated as Original Service Agreement No. LGIA ISONE/VERSANT-26-02 under the ISO Tariff.\n\n8\n\nThe Evergreen III LGIA has been designated as Original Service Agreement No. LGIA ISONE/VERSANT-26-03 under the ISO Tariff.\n\n9\n\nThese agreements were filed by ISO-NE and then Transmission Owner Bangor Hydro in 2009 and accepted by the Commission in Docket No. ER09-1588.       "text": "Interconnection Process Guide\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/getSignInInfo\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/authenticate\n\n# Interconnection Process Guide\n\n### In this section\n\n- Interconnection Process Guide\n- Is the ISO New England Interconnection Process Right for You?\n- Register for an IRTT Account\n- Submit an Interconnection Request During Cluster Request Window\n- Register Your Asset\n\n### Related Links\n\n- Glossary of Interconnection-Related Acronyms\n- Interconnection-Related Governing Documents\n- Contact IRTT Support\n\nISO New England is providing this web guide to assist you in understanding the interconnection process for small and large generating facilities and elective transmission upgrades (ETUs) to interconnect to the administered transmission system. However, you shouldn’t rely solely on this web guide. Consult the ISO New England Inc. Transmission, Markets, and Services Tariff(ISO Tariff), Section II, known as the ISO’s Open Access Transmission Tariff(OATT), and relevant planning procedures and operating procedures to ensure that you are complying with all requirements. In case of any discrepancy with this web guide, the ISO Tariff and applicable operating procedures and planning procedures govern.\n\n## Overview of the Interconnection Process\n\nAs a Regional Transmission Organization (RTO), the ISO coordinates how transmission lines, power plants, and other resources connect to and operate on New England’s administered transmission system to ensure reliability and facilitate wholesale electricity market participation. The ISO does not administer the interconnection process for projects subject to state interconnection processes. Thus, after you determine that the ISO interconnection process applies to your project, you’ll need to submit an interconnection request to the ISO through the Interconnection Request Tracking Tool(IRTT). Existing customers can register for an account using IRTT, while new customers must first or simultaneously obtain an ISO customer ID and access to critical energy infrastructure information.\n\nAs required by the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission’s Order No. 2023, it is only possible to submit interconnection requests during a cluster request window. The next window to submit a complete interconnection request package for the next Cluster Study is expected to be open from October 5, 2026 to November 19, 2026. After the ISO validates your interconnection request and the cluster request window closes, the customer engagement window opens and a cluster study scoping meeting is held. A cluster study scoping meeting will address pertinent information about your project and the interconnection studies the ISO will perform to assess your project’s impact on the power system and the facilities and upgrades potentially needed to interconnect your project per all requirements and standards.\n\nAfter the ISO has completed the necessary study work, you’ll work with the ISO and the transmission owner on an Interconnection Agreement (IA), a formal document detailing the terms and conditions for interconnection service. You must enter into an IA before you can construct or operate a generator or an elective transmission upgrade. Final steps of the interconnection process are to submit final data and register your asset with the ISO.\n\n## Governing Documents\n\nSeveral schedules of the ISO’s Open Access Transmission Tariff contain the rules detailing the interconnection process:\n\nSection II, ISO New England Open Access Transmission Tariff(OATT)\n\n- Helps ensure that all transmission owners and transmission customers have open access to transmission service in New England\n- Details the rights and responsibilities of transmission owners and transmission customers as well as the procedures they must follow and the fees transmission customers must pay to access the transmission system\n- Sets out the transmission system planning process for the region\n\n## Resources and Tools\n\n- ISO Planning Procedure No. 5-6(PP 5-6), Scope of Study for System Impact Studies under the Generation Interconnection Procedures—describes the scope of interconnection studies conducted pursuant to Schedule 22, Schedule 23, and Schedule 25.\n- For additional resources for the Interconnection Process see Interconnection Service.\n- “Interconnection Process” training is available on the Training Materials webpage.\n- See the IRTT public queue. (Log in not necessary.) For information on the public version of the ISO New England Interconnection (ISO Queue), visit the Interconnection Request Queue webpage.\n- See the ISO Interconnection Request Tracking Tool (IRTT) and IRTT User Guide.\n- See the Interconnection Request Studies page to view completed studies for projects listed in the ISO’s Interconnection Request Queue (IR CEII required to view).\n- Interconnection frequently asked questions can be found in Ask ISO.\n- See Order No. 2023 Key Project page to access documents and resources related to the Order No. 2023: Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements.\n- Read ISO-NE’s September 30, 2025, responses to Order Nos. 2023/2023-A transition-related questions\n- Read the Interconnection Request Technical Data Submittal Guidance document for guidance on submitting the technical data required for a Cluster Study.\n\n## Questions\n\nEmail IRTT Participant Support at irtt@iso-ne.com, or contact ISO Participant Support.\n\n## Interconnection Documents\n\n### Filters\n\nFilter this list ↑\n\n### Documents\n\n| A listing of links to documents. | Title and Description | Date | Type | Size |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n\nSorry, no results are available with this choice of filter(s). Please deselect one or more filters.",

      "author": "McDonald, Patricia",
      "text": "APPENDIX 11 LARGE GENERATOR INTERCONNECTION AGREEMENT\n\nTABLE OF CONTENTS\n\nArticle 1 Definitions \nArticle 2 Effective Date, Term and Termination \nArticle 3 Regulatory Filings \nArticle 4 Scope of Service \nArticle 5 Interconnection Facilities Engineering, Procurement, and Construction \nArticle 6 Testing and Inspection \nArticle 7 Metering \nArticle 8 Communications \nArticle 9 Operations \nArticle 10 Maintenance \nArticle 11 Performance Obligation \nArticle 12 Invoice \nArticle 13 Emergencies \nArticle 14 Regulatory Requirements and Governing Law \nArticle 15 Notices \nArticle 16 Force Majeure \nArticle 17 Default \nArticle 18 Indemnity, Consequential Damages and Insurance \nArticle 19 Assignment \nArticle 20 Severability \nArticle 21 Comparability \nArticle 22 Confidentiality \nArticle 23 Environmental Releases \nArticle 24 Information Requirements \nArticle 25 Information Access and Audit Rights \nArticle 26 Subcontractors \nArticle 27 Disputes \nArticle 28 Representations, Warranties and Covenants \n\nArticle 29 Omitted Article 30 Miscellaneous\n\nTHIS STANDARD LARGE GENERATOR INTERCONNECTION AGREEMENT (“Agreement”) is made and entered into this ____ day of ________ 20__, by and between ________________, a ________________ organized and existing under the laws of the State/Commonwealth of ________________ (“Interconnection Customer” with a Large Generating Facility), ISO New England Inc., a non-stock corporation organized and existing under the laws of the State of Delaware (“System Operator”), and ________________, a ________________ organized and existing under the laws of the State/Commonwealth of ________________ (“Interconnecting Transmission Owner”). Under this Agreement, the Interconnection Customer, System Operator, and Interconnecting Transmission Owner each may be referred to as a “Party” or collectively as the “Parties.”\n\nRECITALS\n\nWHEREAS, System Operator is the central dispatching agency provided for under the Transmission Operating Agreement (“TOA”) which has responsibility for the operation of the New England Control Area from the System Operator control center and the administration of the Tariff; and\n\nWHEREAS, Interconnecting Transmission Owner is the owner or possessor of an interest in the Administered Transmission System; and\n\nWHEREAS, Interconnection Customer intends to own, lease and/or control and operate the Generating Facility identified as a Large Generating Facility in Appendix C to this Agreement; and\n\nWHEREAS, System Operator, Interconnection Customer and Interconnecting Transmission Owner have agreed to enter into this Agreement for the purpose of interconnecting the Large Generating Facility to the Administered Transmission System.\n\nNOW, THEREFORE, in consideration of and subject to the mutual covenants contained herein, it is agreed:\n\nWhen used in this Standard Large Generator Interconnection Agreement, terms with initial capitalization that are not defined in Article 1 shall have the meanings specified in the Article in which they are used.\n\nARTICLE 1. DEFINITIONS The definitions contained in this Article 1 and those definitions embedded in an Article of this Agreement are intended to apply in the context of the generator interconnection process provided for in Schedule 22 (and its appendices). To the extent that the definitions herein are different than those contained in Section I.2.2 of the Tariff, the definitions provided below shall control only for purposes of generator interconnections under Schedule 22. Capitalized terms in Schedule 22 that are not defined in this Article 1 shall have the meanings specified in Section I.2.2 of the Tariff.\n\nAdministered Transmission System shall mean the PTF and the Non-PTF.\n\nAdverse System Impact shall mean any significant negative effects on the stability, reliability or operating characteristics of the electric system.\n\nAffected Party shall mean the entity that owns, operates or controls an Affected System, or any other entity operating outside of the New England Control Area that otherwise may be a necessary party to the interconnection process.\n\nAffected System shall mean any electric system that is outside the New England Control Area that may be affected by the proposed interconnection.\n\nAffected System Facilities Construction       "text": "FEBRUARY 15, 2024 | NEPOOL TRANSMISSION COMMITTEE | DOUBLETREE HOTEL, MILFORD, M A\n\nAl McBride D I R E C T O R | T R A N S M I S S I O N S E R V I C E S & R E S O U R C E Q U A L I F I C A T I O N\n\n#### Summary of Tariff Redline Updates since the January TC Meeting\n\n## Order No. 2023 – Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements\n\nISO-NE PUBLIC 2\n\n### Presentation Overview\n\n• ISO has posted an updated draft of all Tariff redlines associated with Order No. 2023 compliance \n• This presentation summarizes the major updates to the Tariff redlines since the January TC meeting \n• Focus on Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (LGIP) in Schedule 22 of the Open Access Transmission Tariff (OATT) – The Small Generator Interconnection Procedures (SGIP) in Schedule 23 of the OATT have been overhauled to mirror the LGIP – The Elective Transmission Upgrade Interconnection Procedures (ETUIP) in Schedule 25 of the OATT also closely match the LGIP \n\nISO-NE PUBLIC ISO-NE INTERNAL USE\n\n### UPDATES TO THE TARIFF REDLINES SINCE THE\n\n### JANUARY TC MEETING\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 3.1\n\nCommercial Readiness Deposits that must be submitted to the System Operator under this LGIP must be (a) delivered to the System Operator’s bank account by electronic transfer, or (b) through the provision and maintenance of an irrevocable lLetter of cCredit in a form and from a financial institution acceptable to System Operator and included on the List of Eligible Letter of Credit Issuers, as described on the System Operator’s public website, or (c) a combination thereof. Each letter of credit must specify the Interconnection Request to which it corresponds. All costs associated with obtaining a letter of credit shall be borne by the Interconnection Customer. In the event that System Operator identifies an administrative deficiency with a submitted letter of credit, Interconnection Customer shall have five (5) Business Days to cure the deficiency..\n\nIn response to stakeholder suggestion, a cure period has been included for certain Letter of Credit (LOC) issues. Note that an LOC, in the correct amount, must have been submitted by the relevant deadline\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 3.1\n\nIf the System Operator removes the financial institution from the list, Interconnection Customer shall have five (5) Business Days from the date on which System Operator provides notice of such removal to replace the letter of credit with a letter of credit from a financial institution on the list. The System Operator may extend this cure period in its sole discretion. Failure to cure a deficiency within the periods prescribed in this Section 3.1 shall result in the withdrawal of the Interconnection Request pursuant to Section 3.7 of the LGIP without further opportunity to cure.\n\nAdded cure period for certain LOC issues. Note that an LOC, in the correct amount, must have been submitted by the relevant deadline\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Definitions\n\nTransitional Capacity Network Resource Group Study (“Transitional CNR Group Study”) shall mean the study performed by the System Operator under Section III.13.1.1.2.3A of the Tariff and Section 5.1.1.3 of this LGIP.\n\nDefinition for the Transitional Capacity Network Resource Group Study\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 3.2.1.3\n\nAny Interconnection Customer with a valid Interconnection Request for CNR Interconnection Service that has a completed Interconnection System Impact Study as of May 1, 2024, but that has not received a Capacity Supply Obligation through eighteenth Forward Capacity Auction or an earlier auction may: 1) seek to complete the process for obtaining CNR Interconnection Service through the process described in Section III.13.1.1.2A of the Tariff or 2) seek to complete the process for obtaining CNR Interconnection Service through the Transitional Cluster Study. Notwithstanding any other provision of the Tariff, an Interconnection Customer may seek to participate in both the process described in Section III.13.1.1.2A of the Tariff and the Transitional Cluster Study simultaneously. If the Interconnection Customer achieves CNR Interconnection Service through Section III.13.1.1.2A, it may withdraw from the Transitional Cluster Study without penalty and be refunded any remaining study deposits associated with the Transitional Cluster Study. If the Interconnection Customer does not enter, or complete, the process described in either Section III.13.1.1.2A or the Transitional Cluster Study, the System Operator shall reduce the Interconnection Customer’s Interconnection Request to NR Interconnection Service.\n\nIn response to Stakeholder suggestion, captures opportunities to establish CNRIS through the Transitional CNR Group Study or Transitional Cluster\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 5.1.1.3\n\nIn accordance with Section III.13.1.1.2.3A, System Operator shall conduct a Transitional CNR Group Study following the effective date of this LGIP. An Interconnection Customer with an assigned Queue Position as of thirty (30) Calendar Days after April 1, 2024 may participate in the Transitional CNR Group Study, and consistent with Section II.48 of the Tariff, achieve CNR Interconnection Service. Any Interconnection Customer seeking to establish CNR Interconnection Service through this study must (1) have a valid Interconnection Request seeking CNR Interconnection Service, (2) submit a New Capacity Show of Interest Form to participate in the interim reconfiguration auction qualification process, (3) have not secured a Capacity Supply Obligation in the eighteenth Forward Capacity Auction or earlier, (4) have a completed System Impact Study or Interconnection Agreement establishing NR Interconnection Service, and 5) have a Commercial Operation Date prior to June 1, 2028.\n\nEligibility for Transitional CNR Group Study\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 5.1.1.3\n\nSystem Operator shall conduct the study by performing an overlapping impacts analysis in the manner used for CNR Group Studies conducted prior to the effective date of this LGIP and as described in ISO Section III.13.1.1.2.3A and the ISO New England Planning Procedures. The Transitional CNR Group Study shall assure that the Interconnection Customer’s Large Generating Facility can be interconnected in a manner that ensures intra-zonal deliverability by avoidance of the redispatch of other Capacity Network Resources and Elective Transmission Upgrades with CNI Interconnection Service, in accordance with the CC Interconnection Standard and as detailed in the ISO New England Planning Procedures.\n\nScope of Transitional CNR Group Study\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 5.1.1.3\n\nInterconnection Requests for CNR Interconnection Service and CNI Interconnection Service submitted to the System Operator pursuant to Schedules 22, 23, or 25 of Section II of the Tariff and interconnection requests submitted to Interconnecting Transmission Owners or their distribution company affiliates pursuant to the applicable state tariff, rules or procedures that have obtained Proposed Plan Application approval from the System Operator pursuant to Section I.3.9 of the Tariff shall be included in the Transitional CNR Group Study in order of submission/approval (the dates of submission shall be used for Interconnection Requests submitted to the System Operator and the dates of Proposed Plan Application approval shall be used for interconnection requests submitted to Interconnecting Transmission Owners or their distribution company affiliates).\n\nScope of Transitional CNR Group Study, continued\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 5.1.1.3\n\nInterconnection requests submitted to Interconnecting Transmission Owners or their distribution company a",

      "text": "UNITED STATES OF AMERICA BEFORE THE FEDERAL ENERGY REGULATORY COMMISSION\n\nImprovements to Generator ) \nInterconnection Procedures and ) Docket No. RM22-14-000 \nAgreements ) \n\nINITIAL COMMENTS OF ISO NEW ENGLAND INC.\n\nISO New England Inc. (“ISO-NE” or “ISO”) respectfully submits these comments in response to the Notice of Proposed Rulemaking on Improvements to Generator Interconnection\n\nProcedures and Agreements that the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission’s (“Commission”)\n\nissued on June 16, 2022, in the above-captioned proceeding. 1\n\nI. EXECUTIVE SUMMARY\n\nThe Commission’s proposed rule is the second effort stemming from its July 15, 2021\n\nAdvanced Notice of Proposed Rulemaking, 2 which sought to examine the need for reforms in regional transmission planning and generator interconnection processes established in Order Nos.\n\n1\n\nSee Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements, 179 FERC ¶ 61,194 (2022) (“NOPR”). New England’s Interconnection Procedures are contained in Schedules 22, 23 and 25 of Section II of the ISO New England Transmission, Markets and Services Tariff (“Tariff”), which contains the Open Access Transmission Tariff (“OATT”). Schedule 22 contains the Large Generator Interconnection Procedures and Interconnection Agreement (“Schedule 22” or “LGIP”); Schedule 23 contains the Small Generator Interconnection Procedures and Interconnection Agreement (“Schedule 23” or “SGIP”), and Schedule 25 contains the Elective Transmission Upgrade Interconnection Procedures and Interconnection Agreement (“Schedule 25” or “ETU IP”).\n\n2\n\nBuilding for the Future Through Electric Regional Transmission Planning and Cost Allocation and Generator Interconnection, Advance Notice of Proposed Rulemaking, 176 FERC ¶ 61,024 (July 15, 2021) (“ANOPR”); Initial Comments of ISO New Inc., Docket No. RM21-17-000 (Oct. 12, 2021) (“ANOPR Comments”).\n\n2003, 890, and 1000. 3 In the NOPR, the Commission proposes to: “(1) implement a first-ready, first-served cluster study process, (2) increase the speed of interconnection queue processing, and (3) incorporate technological advancements into the interconnection process,” for the Large\n\nGenerating Facilities. 4 The Commission further proposes to “incorporate alternative transmission technologies into the interconnection process and to provide modeling and performance requirements for non-synchronous generators” for Small Generating Facilities. 5\n\nIn New England, the states have set aggressive decarbonization goals, which are expected to drive the dramatic transformation of the resource mix in the region from conventional generators to renewable resources, and increase demand through the electrification of the economy. 6\n\nAchieving this clean energy transition safely and reliably depends, in part, on an efficient interconnection process. Based on the ISO’s interconnection queue as of March 2022, the future resource mix proposed to meet the increasing demand comprises 96% renewable resources, totaling approximately 30,000 MW, including approximately 20,000 MW of wind facilities. 7\n\nThanks to significant Tariff improvements proposed jointly with the New England Participant\n\nTransmission Owners (“PTOs”) and the New England Power Pool (“NEPOOL”), and supported by the New England states, the region’s existing Interconnection Procedures are facilitating\n\n3\n\nStandardization of Generator Interconnection Agreements and Procedures, 104 FERC ¶ 61,103 (2003) (“Order No. 2003”); Preventing Undue Discrimination and Preference in Transmission Service, 118 FERC ¶ 61,119 (2007) (“Order No. 890”); Transmission Planning and Cost Allocation by Transmission Owning and Operating Public Utilities, 136 FERC ¶ 61,051 (2011) (“Order No. 1000”).\n\n4\n\nNOPR at P 4.\n\n5\n\nId. at P 5.\n\n6\n\nFor additional details, see ISO New England Inc.’s 2022 Regional Electric Outlook, available on the ISO NE website at https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/2022/06/2022_reo.pdf (“2022 REO”).\n\n7\n\nSee 2022 REO at 11, 15.\n\ndevelopment of significant amounts of renewable generation. The ISO, however, agrees with the\n\nCommission’s statements in the NOPR that the process can be more efficient. 8\n\nAs discussed in Sections III and IV below, many of the interconnection reforms that ISO\n\nNE has implemented since Order Nos. 2003 and 2006 are consistent with the spirit of the\n\nCommission’s proposed interconnection reforms in the NOPR. These include improvements to integrate the interconnection queue with the Forward Capacity Market (“FCM”), incorporate\n\nElective Transmission Upgrades (“ETU”) into the interconnection process, and establish a limited\n\ncluster study process.\n\nAccordingly, ISO-NE respectfully requests that the Commission not adopt uniform or prescriptive compliance requirements that fail to account for these regional variations in any final rule issued in this proceeding. Instead, the ISO requests that the Commission recognize existing regional differences and allow the necessary flexibility for the ISO to develop a compliance approach that builds on the region’s accomplishments, consistent with the Commission’s long standing principles. This will allow for incorporation of the elements proposed by the Commission into existing processes and procedures in a way that best meets the needs of the region.\n\nWith respect to the specific reforms proposed in the NOPR, ISO-NE offers the following\n\ncomments, discussed in further detail below:\n\n The NOPR appropriately recognizes that significant trade-offs are involved when \npursuing a change as expansive as the first-ready, first-served construct proposed. \nUnder the proposal, ISO-NE’s study efforts would be focused on fewer projects \nthat are more likely to be constructed. However, this trade-off must be realistic. \n Higher study completion expectations and the elimination of the reasonable efforts \nstandard would require that study effort be focused on projects that are sufficiently \nviable, necessitate less flexibility for midstream project changes/ability to consider \nalternatives, and require stricter expectations for the completion of all aspects of \nthe process, such as the preparation of transmission upgrade cost-estimates. \n\nSee e.g. NOPR at PP 3, 22.\n\n New England has made several enhancements to the Interconnection Procedures in \nrecent years including the integration of the interconnection process with the FCM, \nthe inclusion of a process for ETUs, and targeted clustering. The Commission \nshould respect these enhancements in any final rule and allow the ISO to build upon \nthem for compliance purposes. \n The first-ready, first-served construct should apply to all interconnection \nprocedures, not just to large generators, to avoid the operation of multiple, separate \nqueues with fundamentally different expectations in terms of studies and \ntimeframes. \n\nII. COMMUNICATIONS\n\nISO-NE is the independent, private, nonprofit entity that serves as the Regional\n\nTransmission Organization (“RTO”) for New England. The ISO operates the New England bulk power system and administers New England’s organized wholesale electricity market pursuant to the Tariff and the Transmission Operating Agreement (“TOA”) with the New England\n\nParticipating Transmission Owners (“PTO”). As the RTO, the ISO has the responsibility to protect the short-term reliability of the New England Control Area and to plan and operate the system according to reliability standards established by the ISO, the Northeast Power Coordinating\n\nCouncil, Inc. (“NPCC”) and the North American Electric Reliability Corporation (“NERC”).\n\nCorrespondence and communications in this proceeding should be addressed to:\n\nGraham Jesmer \nISO New England Inc. \nOne Sullivan Road \nHolyoke, MA 01040-2841 \nTel: (413) 540-4557 \nFax: (413) 535-4379 \nE-mail: gjesmer@iso-ne.com \n\nIII.       "text": "Interconnection Process Guide\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/getSignInInfo\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/authenticate\n\n# Interconnection Process Guide\n\n### In this section\n\n- Interconnection Process Guide\n- Is the ISO New England Interconnection Process Right for You?\n- Register for an IRTT Account\n- Submit an Interconnection Request During Cluster Request Window\n- Register Your Asset\n\n### Related Links\n\n- Glossary of Interconnection-Related Acronyms\n- Interconnection-Related Governing Documents\n- Contact IRTT Support\n\nISO New England is providing this web guide to assist you in understanding the interconnection process for small and large generating facilities and elective transmission upgrades (ETUs) to interconnect to the administered transmission system. However, you shouldn’t rely solely on this web guide. Consult the ISO New England Inc. Transmission, Markets, and Services Tariff(ISO Tariff), Section II, known as the ISO’s Open Access Transmission Tariff(OATT), and relevant planning procedures and operating procedures to ensure that you are complying with all requirements. In case of any discrepancy with this web guide, the ISO Tariff and applicable operating procedures and planning procedures govern.\n\n## Overview of the Interconnection Process\n\nAs a Regional Transmission Organization (RTO), the ISO coordinates how transmission lines, power plants, and other resources connect to and operate on New England’s administered transmission system to ensure reliability and facilitate wholesale electricity market participation. The ISO does not administer the interconnection process for projects subject to state interconnection processes. Thus, after you determine that the ISO interconnection process applies to your project, you’ll need to submit an interconnection request to the ISO through the Interconnection Request Tracking Tool(IRTT). Existing customers can register for an account using IRTT, while new customers must first or simultaneously obtain an ISO customer ID and access to critical energy infrastructure information.\n\nAs required by the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission’s Order No. 2023, it is only possible to submit interconnection requests during a cluster request window. The next window to submit a complete interconnection request package for the next Cluster Study is expected to be open from October 5, 2026 to November 19, 2026. After the ISO validates your interconnection request and the cluster request window closes, the customer engagement window opens and a cluster study scoping meeting is held. A cluster study scoping meeting will address pertinent information about your project and the interconnection studies the ISO will perform to assess your project’s impact on the power system and the facilities and upgrades potentially needed to interconnect your project per all requirements and standards.\n\nAfter the ISO has completed the necessary study work, you’ll work with the ISO and the transmission owner on an Interconnection Agreement (IA), a formal document detailing the terms and conditions for interconnection service. You must enter into an IA before you can construct or operate a generator or an elective transmission upgrade. Final steps of the interconnection process are to submit final data and register your asset with the ISO.\n\n## Governing Documents\n\nSeveral schedules of the ISO’s Open Access Transmission Tariff contain the rules detailing the interconnection process:\n\nSection II, ISO New England Open Access Transmission Tariff(OATT)\n\n- Helps ensure that all transmission owners and transmission customers have open access to transmission service in New England\n- Details the rights and responsibilities of transmission owners and transmission customers as well as the procedures they must follow and the fees transmission customers must pay to access the transmission system\n- Sets out the transmission system planning process for the region\n\n## Resources and Tools\n\n- ISO Planning Procedure No. 5-6(PP 5-6), Scope of Study for System Impact Studies under the Generation Interconnection Procedures—describes the scope of interconnection studies conducted pursuant to Schedule 22, Schedule 23, and Schedule 25.\n- For additional resources for the Interconnection Process see Interconnection Service.\n- “Interconnection Process” training is available on the Training Materials webpage.\n- See the IRTT public queue. (Log in not necessary.) For information on the public version of the ISO New England Interconnection (ISO Queue), visit the Interconnection Request Queue webpage.\n- See the ISO Interconnection Request Tracking Tool (IRTT) and IRTT User Guide.\n- See the Interconnection Request Studies page to view completed studies for projects listed in the ISO’s Interconnection Request Queue (IR CEII required to view).\n- Interconnection frequently asked questions can be found in Ask ISO.\n- See Order No. 2023 Key Project page to access documents and resources related to the Order No. 2023: Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements.\n- Read ISO-NE’s September 30, 2025, responses to Order Nos. 2023/2023-A transition-related questions\n- Read the Interconnection Request Technical Data Submittal Guidance document for guidance on submitting the technical data required for a Cluster Study.\n\n## Questions\n\nEmail IRTT Participant Support at irtt@iso-ne.com, or contact ISO Participant Support.\n\n## Interconnection Documents\n\n### Filters\n\nFilter this list ↑\n\n### Documents\n\n| A listing of links to documents. | Title and Description | Date | Type | Size |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n\nSorry, no results are available with this choice of filter(s). Please deselect one or more filters.",

{

  "data": "{
  "query": "ISO New England Schedule 22 LGIP Section 3.4 Valid Interconnection Request requirements fees deposit",
  "num_results": 8,
  "results": [
    {
      "url": "https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100008/a03b_2024_02_15_tc_order2023_redlines_update_presentation.pdf",
      "title": "Order No. 2023 – Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements",
      "text": "FEBRUARY 15, 2024 | NEPOOL TRANSMISSION COMMITTEE | DOUBLETREE HOTEL, MILFORD, M A\n\nAl McBride D I R E C T O R | T R A N S M I S S I O N S E R V I C E S & R E S O U R C E Q U A L I F I C A T I O N\n\n#### Summary of Tariff Redline Updates since the January TC Meeting\n\n## Order No. 2023 – Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements\n\nISO-NE PUBLIC 2\n\n### Presentation Overview\n\n• ISO has posted an updated draft of all Tariff redlines associated with Order No. 2023 compliance \n• This presentation summarizes the major updates to the Tariff redlines since the January TC meeting \n• Focus on Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (LGIP) in Schedule 22 of the Open Access Transmission Tariff (OATT) – The Small Generator Interconnection Procedures (SGIP) in Schedule 23 of the OATT have been overhauled to mirror the LGIP – The Elective Transmission Upgrade Interconnection Procedures (ETUIP) in Schedule 25 of the OATT also closely match the LGIP \n\nISO-NE PUBLIC ISO-NE INTERNAL USE\n\n### UPDATES TO THE TARIFF REDLINES SINCE THE\n\n### JANUARY TC MEETING\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 3.1\n\nCommercial Readiness Deposits that must be submitted to the System Operator under this LGIP must be (a) delivered to the System Operator’s bank account by electronic transfer, or (b) through the provision and maintenance of an irrevocable lLetter of cCredit in a form and from a financial institution acceptable to System Operator and included on the List of Eligible Letter of Credit Issuers, as described on the System Operator’s public website, or (c) a combination thereof. Each letter of credit must specify the Interconnection Request to which it corresponds. All costs associated with obtaining a letter of credit shall be borne by the Interconnection Customer. In the event that System Operator identifies an administrative deficiency with a submitted letter of credit, Interconnection Customer shall have five (5) Business Days to cure the deficiency..\n\nIn response to stakeholder suggestion, a cure period has been included for certain Letter of Credit (LOC) issues. Note that an LOC, in the correct amount, must have been submitted by the relevant deadline\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 3.1\n\nIf the System Operator removes the financial institution from the list, Interconnection Customer shall have five (5) Business Days from the date on which System Operator provides notice of such removal to replace the letter of credit with a letter of credit from a financial institution on the list. The System Operator may extend this cure period in its sole discretion. Failure to cure a deficiency within the periods prescribed in this Section 3.1 shall result in the withdrawal of the Interconnection Request pursuant to Section 3.7 of the LGIP without further opportunity to cure.\n\nAdded cure period for certain LOC issues. Note that an LOC, in the correct amount, must have been submitted by the relevant deadline\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Definitions\n\nTransitional Capacity Network Resource Group Study (“Transitional CNR Group Study”) shall mean the study performed by the System Operator under Section III.13.1.1.2.3A of the Tariff and Section 5.1.1.3 of this LGIP.\n\nDefinition for the Transitional Capacity Network Resource Group Study\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 3.2.1.3\n\nAny Interconnection Customer with a valid Interconnection Request for CNR Interconnection Service that has a completed Interconnection System Impact Study as of May 1, 2024, but that has not received a Capacity Supply Obligation through eighteenth Forward Capacity Auction or an earlier auction may: 1) seek to complete the process for obtaining CNR Interconnection Service through the process described in Section III.13.1.1.2A of the Tariff or 2) seek to complete the process for obtaining CNR Interconnection Service through the Transitional Cluster Study. Notwithstanding any other provision of the Tariff, an Interconnection Customer may seek to participate in both the process described in Section III.13.1.1.2A of the Tariff and the Transitional Cluster Study simultaneously. If the Interconnection Customer achieves CNR Interconnection Service through Section III.13.1.1.2A, it may withdraw from the Transitional Cluster Study without penalty and be refunded any remaining study deposits associated with the Transitional Cluster Study. If the Interconnection Customer does not enter, or complete, the process described in either Section III.13.1.1.2A or the Transitional Cluster Study, the System Operator shall reduce the Interconnection Customer’s Interconnection Request to NR Interconnection Service.\n\nIn response to Stakeholder suggestion, captures opportunities to establish CNRIS through the Transitional CNR Group Study or Transitional Cluster\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 5.1.1.3\n\nIn accordance with Section III.13.1.1.2.3A, System Operator shall conduct a Transitional CNR Group Study following the effective date of this LGIP. An Interconnection Customer with an assigned Queue Position as of thirty (30) Calendar Days after April 1, 2024 may participate in the Transitional CNR Group Study, and consistent with Section II.48 of the Tariff, achieve CNR Interconnection Service. Any Interconnection Customer seeking to establish CNR Interconnection Service through this study must (1) have a valid Interconnection Request seeking CNR Interconnection Service, (2) submit a New Capacity Show of Interest Form to participate in the interim reconfiguration auction qualification process, (3) have not secured a Capacity Supply Obligation in the eighteenth Forward Capacity Auction or earlier, (4) have a completed System Impact Study or Interconnection Agreement establishing NR Interconnection Service, and 5) have a Commercial Operation Date prior to June 1, 2028.\n\nEligibility for Transitional CNR Group Study\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 5.1.1.3\n\nSystem Operator shall conduct the study by performing an overlapping impacts analysis in the manner used for CNR Group Studies conducted prior to the effective date of this LGIP and as described in ISO Section III.13.1.1.2.3A and the ISO New England Planning Procedures. The Transitional CNR Group Study shall assure that the Interconnection Customer’s Large Generating Facility can be interconnected in a manner that ensures intra-zonal deliverability by avoidance of the redispatch of other Capacity Network Resources and Elective Transmission Upgrades with CNI Interconnection Service, in accordance with the CC Interconnection Standard and as detailed in the ISO New England Planning Procedures.\n\nScope of Transitional CNR Group Study\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 5.1.1.3\n\nInterconnection Requests for CNR Interconnection Service and CNI Interconnection Service submitted to the System Operator pursuant to Schedules 22, 23, or 25 of Section II of the Tariff and interconnection requests submitted to Interconnecting Transmission Owners or their distribution company affiliates pursuant to the applicable state tariff, rules or procedures that have obtained Proposed Plan Application approval from the System Operator pursuant to Section I.3.9 of the Tariff shall be included in the Transitional CNR Group Study in order of submission/approval (the dates of submission shall be used for Interconnection Requests submitted to the System Operator and the dates of Proposed Plan Application approval shall be used for interconnection requests submitted to Interconnecting Transmission Owners or their distribution company affiliates).\n\nScope of Transitional CNR Group Study, continued\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 5.1.1.3\n\nInterconnection requests submitted to Interconnecting Transmission Owners or their distribution company a",
